lines: ENGINEERING DESIGN PROJECT · ESTONIAN AVIATION ACADEMY · 2025
FIA-Compliant Hobby Kart — 3D CAD Design Report
Hendrik Tiirats · Mikk Eke Kalamees · Kristjan Sarapuu
A  team  of  three  engineering  students  at  the  Estonian  Aviation  Academy  designed  and  modelled  a
complete  FIA-compliant  hobby  kart  in  SolidWorks  —  producing  a  fully  constrained  32-part  assembly
with technical drawings, a bill of materials, and a detailed cost estimate.
The  project's  primary  objective  was  to  design  and  model  a  FIA-compliant  hobby  kart  using  SolidWorks,  with  a
focus on structural integrity, component interoperability, and design for maintainability. A secondary objective was
to  develop  the  team's  capability  to  analyse  and  critically  evaluate  engineering  design  decisions  through  formal
The deliverables comprised a fully constrained SolidWorks assembly model encompassing chassis construction, a
basic  suspension  layout,  a  steering  system,  and  a  braking  system  —  together  with  engineering  drawings
conforming to ISO standards and a supporting technical report.
Before  beginning  detailed  design,  the  team  evaluated  five  candidate  vehicle  concepts  against  three  weighted
criteria: cost (40%), project scope (40%), and time (20%). Scores were assigned on a 1–5 scale; the weighted total
Tasks  were  distributed  at  project  inception  using  a  responsibility-assignment  framework:  each  member  owned
specific  subsystems  end-to-end  (design  (cid:174)  model  (cid:174)  assembly  integration),  while  retaining  the  right  to  flag  and
improve any part of the shared assembly. Final sign-off on each subsystem rested with its nominated owner.
Chassis frame  ·  Steering system & column  ·  Simplified engine representation  ·  Wheels / rims
Driver's seat  ·  Drive system  ·  Pedals & pedal mounts  ·  Floor plate  ·  Body panels / bumpers
Braking system (callipers, disc, pads, master cylinder)  ·  Wheels, hubs & tyres
The  complete  assembly  comprises  32  unique  part  types  across  all  subsystems.  Every  part  was  modelled  as  a
discrete SolidWorks component and mated into the top-level assembly with fully defined geometric constraints.
The  cost  estimate  reflects  FIA-compliant  components  and  manufacturing  processes.  All  major  cost  drivers  —
chassis  fabrication,  powertrain,  suspension,  safety  hardware,  and  steering  —  were  costed  independently.  The
engine cost assumes a new four-stroke unit from a recognised supplier (e.g. Rotax), inclusive of clutch and primary
Compliant with FIA stiffness & safety requirements
Inclusive of clutch and primary power transmission
Rear axle, brake system, rims, tyres
Seat harness, bumpers, fuel tank, brake reliability components
Steering system, drive chain, fasteners, fittings
The  finalised  assembly  was  rendered  from  two  orthographic  perspectives  to  demonstrate  the  overall  layout  and
visual coherence of the design. Total assembly mass: 102.7 kg.
Rear isometric view — drivetrain and brake disc visible
Front isometric view — steering geometry and pedal layout
Top-level assembly drawing — all bearings and fasteners sized to shaft/hole dimensions. Total mass: 102.72 kg. Scale 1:13.
Engineering drawings were produced for all custom-designed components in accordance with ISO 2768-f general
tolerancing.  The  drawings  below  represent  a  cross-section  of  the  work  produced  across  all  three  subsystem
Front Tyre · Rubber · Ø140 bore / Ø260 OD / Ø280 section · Mass 4.58
Brake Disc · AISI 1045 Steel, cold-drawn · Ø220 / Ø160 / t=12 mm · 10×
M6 bolt pattern · Mass 1.42 kg · Scale 1:3
Rear Axle · Alloy Steel · L=1040 mm / Ø56 / t=3 mm wall · Mass 3.68 kg ·
Steering Tie-Rod End · Alloy Steel · L=170 mm / Ø14–Ø20 section ·
Steering Shaft Clamp · Alloy Steel · 72 mm × 16 mm · 2× M6 THRU ALL
All  project  objectives  were  met.  A  complete,  fully  constrained  SolidWorks  assembly  was  delivered  —
encompassing chassis, drivetrain, steering, braking, and body panels — together with ISO-standard engineering
drawings and a documented cost model. The assembly is publicly accessible via the 3DExperience platform.
The  project  deepened  the  team's  understanding  of  kart  architecture  and  the  interdependencies  between
subsystems — particularly the geometric constraints linking steering geometry, wheel hub sizing, and brake disc
fitment. SolidWorks proficiency improved substantially across all members, from basic part modelling to complex
A  deliberate  scope  boundary  was  maintained:  structural  FEA  (finite  element  analysis)  and  detailed  load
calculations were out of scope for this phase. The 3D model provides a solid foundation for future stress analysis,
prototype fabrication, or further development of any individual subsystem.
SolidWorks part modelling, assembly management, drawing sheet creation, ISO tolerancing
FIA regulation compliance, design-for-maintainability, system decomposition
Work-breakdown structure, responsibility assignment, peer review process
Technical CommunicationEngineering drawings to ISO 2768-f, formal report writing, cost estimation

Supervisor: Mailis Zimmermann · Modelled in SolidWorks · Assembly available on 3DExperience
